The D-Day beaches and Bayeux Tapestry, further out
cherbourg_france.tip: The Cité de la Mer is right by the ship and genuinely excellent — an easy, weather-proof day. For history buffs, the D-Day beaches and Bayeux are the bigger draw an hour away.

bordeaux.blurb: Bordeaux is a rare port where the ship sails up the Garonne into the heart of a UNESCO city — grand 18th-century stone façades, the mirror-pool Miroir d'eau, and of course the wine, with the Cité du Vin museum and châteaux beyond.
miss: The Place de la Bourse and its reflecting Miroir d'eau
La Cité du Vin wine museum
A Médoc or Saint-Émilion vineyard tour
bordeaux.tip: You dock right in the city, so Bordeaux itself is walkable — but this is the one port where a half-day wine-château excursion is almost obligatory. Book Saint-Émilion if you can.

la-rochelle.blurb: La Rochelle is one of France's prettiest Atlantic ports, its old harbour guarded by medieval stone towers and ringed by arcaded streets and seafood restaurants. The vast Aquarium and the Île de Ré's beaches are close.
miss: The medieval harbour towers you can climb
The old town's arcaded shopping streets
La Rochelle Aquarium, one of Europe's best
la-rochelle.tip: The old port and walled towers are a short, flat walk from the berth — an easy, charming day on foot, with the aquarium a good rainy-weather backup.

genoa_italy.blurb: Genoa is a proud, gritty maritime republic-turned-city, its UNESCO Palazzi dei Rolli lining lanes so narrow they never see sun. The huge old port, redesigned by Renzo Piano, holds Europe's biggest aquarium.
miss: The Palazzi dei Rolli on Via Garibaldi
The Aquarium of Genoa at the old port
The caruggi — the labyrinthine medieval alleys
genoa_italy.tip: Genoa is also a gateway to the Cinque Terre and Portofino, but both are long day-trips — if you stay in the city, the Rolli palaces and the caruggi are an atmospheric, underrated day.

savona_italy.blurb: Savona is a Ligurian port and Costa Cruises' home base, with a handsome medieval core around the Priamàr fortress. It's also a springboard to the Riviera — Genoa, and the villages of the coast are within reach by train.
miss: The Priamàr Fortress on the waterfront
The old town's towers and cathedral
Genoa or the Riviera coast by train
savona_italy.tip: Savona is pleasant but modest — the frequent coastal train opens up Genoa in 45 minutes or the Riviera villages, making more of the day than the town alone offers.

la_spezia_italy.blurb: La Spezia is the gateway to the Cinque Terre — the five impossibly picturesque cliff-clinging villages that are the whole reason ships call here. The town itself is workmanlike; the coast is the magic.
miss: The five Cinque Terre villages by train
Portovenere's colourful harbour, just south
Riomaggiore and Vernazza for the classic views
la_spezia_italy.tip: Buy a Cinque Terre train card at La Spezia station and hop between the villages — it's the easiest way to see several. Go early; these tiny places are overwhelmed by midday.

livorno-florence-pisa.blurb: Livorno is Tuscany's port — the gateway to Renaissance Florence and the leaning tower of Pisa. The port city itself has canals and a superb seafood tradition, but nearly everyone heads inland to the art cities.
miss: Florence's Duomo, Uffizi and Michelangelo's David
Pisa's Leaning Tower and Field of Miracles
Livorno's own canals and cacciucco fish stew
livorno-florence-pisa.tip: Florence is 90 minutes each way — pick either Florence OR Pisa plus a Tuscan town, not both. Book Uffizi/Accademia tickets ahead; the queues without them devour a port day.

florence_livorno_italy.blurb: Reached from the port of Livorno, Florence is the cradle of the Renaissance — Brunelleschi's dome, the Uffizi's masterpieces, Michelangelo's David and the goldsmith-lined Ponte Vecchio, all in a compact, walkable centre.
miss: The Duomo and Brunelleschi's dome
The Uffizi Gallery (pre-book to skip the line)
Michelangelo's David at the Accademia
florence_livorno_italy.tip: It's 90 minutes from Livorno, so you get roughly 4–5 hours in the city — enough for the Duomo, one major gallery and a wander, if you've pre-booked entries. Don't attempt Pisa as well.

rome_civitavecchia_italy.blurb: Civitavecchia is the port for Rome, 80km away — the Colosseum, the Vatican, the Trevi Fountain and 2,500 years of history. It's a long but unmissable haul for a first-timer; repeat visitors sometimes explore closer Cerveteri or Tarquinia.
miss: The Colosseum and Roman Forum
The Vatican Museums and St Peter's
The Trevi Fountain and Pantheon
rome_civitavecchia_italy.tip: Rome is a committed day — the train takes 70–80 minutes each way. Pick a focused itinerary (ancient Rome OR the Vatican, not both), pre-book, and watch the all-aboard time religiously.

naples_italy.blurb: Naples is chaotic, historic and thrilling — birthplace of pizza, guardian of Pompeii, and gateway to the Amalfi Coast and Capri, all under the brooding cone of Vesuvius. The ship docks right by the walkable old centre.
miss: Pompeii or Herculaneum's frozen Roman cities
The Amalfi Coast or the island of Capri
Naples' own historic centre and true Neapolitan pizza
naples_italy.tip: So many great options radiate from here that the mistake is trying to combine them — choose one (Pompeii, Amalfi, Capri, or the city) and do it well. For the city itself, the archaeological museum houses Pompeii's best finds.

messina_sicily.blurb: Messina is Sicily's gateway across the strait from the mainland, best known for its astronomical clock's noon performance and as the launch point for Taormina's clifftop beauty and Mount Etna's smoking heights.
miss: Taormina's Greek theatre and clifftop views
Mount Etna's lava fields and craters
Messina's animated astronomical clock at noon
messina_sicily.tip: Taormina is the crowd-pleaser and only 45 minutes away — its Greek theatre framing Etna is one of Sicily's great sights. Be in the cathedral square at noon first if you want the famous clock show.

palermo_sicily.blurb: Palermo is Sicily at its most intense — Arab-Norman palaces and mosaics, baroque churches, street markets that feel like souks, and the mosaic-drenched cathedral of nearby Monreale. Grand, faded and gloriously alive.
miss: Monreale Cathedral's golden mosaics, just outside town
The Palatine Chapel and Norman Palace
The Ballarò or Vucciria street markets
palermo_sicily.tip: Monreale's mosaics are arguably Sicily's single greatest sight and only 20 minutes away — pair them with a walk through Palermo's markets for the full sensory hit.

cagliari_sardinia.blurb: Cagliari, Sardinia's capital, rises in tiers to the walled Castello district with sweeping views over the gulf. A long white beach, Roman remains and pink-flamingo lagoons sit within easy reach of the port.
miss: The hilltop Castello quarter and its bastion terrace
Poetto Beach's long white sands
The Roman amphitheatre and flamingo lagoons
cagliari_sardinia.tip: The old Castello district is a steep but rewarding walk up from the port; a lift eases part of the climb. Poetto Beach is a short bus ride if you'd rather swim than sightsee.

olbia_sardinia.blurb: Olbia is the gateway to Sardinia's glamorous Costa Smeralda — the emerald-water coves and celebrity marinas of Porto Cervo. The town itself is modest; the beaches and the granite-sculpted coastline are the reason to call.
miss: The Costa Smeralda's beaches and Porto Cervo
A boat trip to the Maddalena archipelago
Olbia's San Simplicio basilica in town
olbia_sardinia.tip: The best beaches need transport from Olbia — arrange a taxi or tour to the Costa Smeralda coves, as the town beach doesn't compare to what's a short drive north.

bari.blurb: Bari is the capital of Puglia, its atmospheric old town (Bari Vecchia) a maze where grandmothers make orecchiette pasta in the street. The basilica holds the relics of St Nicholas — Santa Claus himself — and the trulli of Alberobello lie inland.
miss: Bari Vecchia and the Basilica di San Nicola
Watching orecchiette made on Strada Arco Basso
The fairytale trulli houses of Alberobello
bari.tip: Bari's old town is compact and free to wander — see the pasta-makers and the basilica on foot, then decide if the trulli of Alberobello (an hour away) are worth the trip out.

trieste.blurb: Trieste is a grand, Habsburg-flavoured city at Italy's far northeast corner, its vast sea-facing Piazza Unità one of Europe's finest squares. Literary cafés, the clifftop Miramare Castle and a Central-European soul set it apart.
miss: Piazza Unità d'Italia, open to the sea
Miramare Castle on its promontory
A historic Viennese-style coffee house
trieste.tip: Trieste is a walkable, uncrowded pleasure — do the great square and the old town on foot, then take a short bus or boat to the fairytale Miramare Castle on the coast.

venice_italy.blurb: Venice needs no introduction — St Mark's Basilica and its square, the Grand Canal, the Rialto and a thousand bridges over silent water. Large ships now dock at Marghera with a transfer, but the city remains singular.
miss: St Mark's Basilica and the Campanile view
A vaporetto down the Grand Canal
Getting deliberately lost in the quiet back sestieri
venice_italy.tip: Check where your ship berths — big ships use Marghera with a shuttle/boat transfer into the city, adding time. Once in, the vaporetto down the Grand Canal is the best-value 'tour' in Venice.

ljubljana-koper.blurb: Reached from the port of Koper, Ljubljana is one of Europe's most charming small capitals — a car-free riverside centre of pastel façades, dragon-guarded bridges and a hilltop castle, all designed with the architect Plečnik's flair.
miss: The old town and Plečnik's Triple Bridge
Ljubljana Castle's funicular and views
The riverside cafés and central market
ljubljana-koper.tip: Ljubljana is about 90 minutes from Koper — well worth it, and the compact centre is easily walked. Many tours combine it with Lake Bled, but that makes for a very full day.

zadar.blurb: Zadar is a Dalmatian old town on a walled peninsula, layered with Roman ruins and Venetian churches — and two modern marvels, the wave-powered Sea Organ and the solar Sun Salutation on its waterfront.
miss: The Sea Organ playing with the waves
The Roman Forum and round St Donatus church
A day-trip to Plitvice Lakes' waterfalls
zadar.tip: Zadar's old town is walkable straight off the ship — save the sunset-famous Sea Organ for whenever you're there, as it plays all day. Plitvice's lakes are stunning but a long inland haul.

split_croatia.blurb: Split is built inside a Roman emperor's retirement palace — Diocletian's 1,700-year-old walls now enclose a living old town of cafés, apartments and shops. The waterfront Riva and the islands offshore complete it.
miss: Diocletian's Palace and its cellars
The cathedral bell tower's climb and view
The Riva promenade and Marjan hill
split_croatia.tip: The palace is the old town, and it's right at the berth — just walk in and wander. A guided hour helps decode the Roman layers, after which you can explore freely.

dubrovnik_croatia.blurb: Dubrovnik's walled old town is a marble-streeted marvel above the Adriatic — the 'Pearl of the Adriatic', and King's Landing to a generation of TV viewers. Walking the medieval walls is the essential, unforgettable experience.
miss: The circuit of the city walls
The marble Stradun and Rector's Palace
The cable car up Mount Srđ for the aerial view
dubrovnik_croatia.tip: Walk the walls early — they get scorching and jammed by midday, with little shade. The port is 3km from the old town, so use the shuttle or a taxi, not your feet, to get there.

kotor_montenegro.blurb: Kotor sits at the head of a fjord-like bay ringed by mountains, its medieval walled town climbing into a dramatic fortress on the cliff above. The sail in through the Bay of Kotor is one of the Med's most beautiful.
miss: The climb to San Giovanni fortress above the town
The walled old town's squares and cathedral
The Bay of Kotor sail-in from deck
kotor_montenegro.tip: The fortress climb is 1,350 steps and brutally hot after mid-morning — start early with water, or enjoy the walled town at sea level, which is a delight in itself.

corfu_greece.blurb: Corfu's UNESCO old town is a Venetian, French and British layer-cake — elegant arcades, two hilltop fortresses and a cricket-playing esplanade, all wrapped in Ionian green. Beaches and Achilleion palace lie beyond.
miss: The Old Fortress and the Liston arcade
The Achilleion Palace of Empress Sisi
Paleokastritsa's coves on the west coast
corfu_greece.tip: The old town is a short shuttle or 20-minute walk from the port and easily explored on foot — save transport for the beaches or the Achilleion if you want to venture out.

athens_piraeus_greece.blurb: Piraeus is the port for Athens — the Acropolis and its Parthenon crowning the city, the ancient Agora below, and the superb Acropolis Museum at its foot. The cradle of Western civilisation, about 30–40 minutes from the ship.
miss: The Acropolis and Parthenon
The Acropolis Museum's Parthenon gallery
The Plaka old quarter beneath the rock
athens_piraeus_greece.tip: The metro runs from Piraeus straight to the Acropolis area in about 30 minutes for a couple of euros. Go to the Acropolis first thing — the marble bakes and the crowds swell fast by late morning.

mykonos_greece.blurb: Mykonos is the Cyclades at their most glamorous — a dazzling white-and-blue maze of a town, the row of windmills above it, and Little Venice's houses standing in the sea. Chic, crowded and photogenic at every turn.
miss: The iconic windmills above the town
Little Venice's waterfront houses at sunset
Getting lost in Chora's whitewashed lanes
mykonos_greece.tip: Chora's lanes were deliberately built as a maze to confuse pirates and they'll confuse you too — that's the fun; just wander. Beaches need a bus or taxi from town.

santorini_greece.blurb: Santorini is the caldera showstopper — whitewashed towns clinging to volcanic cliffs high above a flooded crater, with Oia's blue domes the most photographed sight in Greece. The tender-and-cable-car access is the catch.
miss: Oia's blue domes and famous sunset views
The clifftop caldera walk from Fira to Firostefani
A boat trip to the volcanic hot springs
santorini_greece.tip: It's a tender port and the cable car from the old port to Fira gets huge queues both ways — go up early, and budget real time for the return line so you don't miss all-aboard.

heraklion_crete_greece.blurb: Heraklion is Crete's capital and the gateway to Knossos, the labyrinthine Bronze Age palace of the Minoans — Europe's oldest city. The town's archaeological museum holds the treasures found there.
miss: The Palace of Knossos and its frescoes
The Heraklion Archaeological Museum's Minoan finds
The Venetian harbour fortress and old town
heraklion_crete_greece.tip: Knossos makes far more sense paired with the archaeological museum, where the original frescoes and artefacts live — do the palace first, then the museum to see what filled it.

rhodes_greece.blurb: Rhodes has the largest inhabited medieval old town in Europe — a walled crusader city of the Knights of St John, with the cobbled Street of the Knights and a Grand Master's palace, all a short walk from the ship.
miss: The Palace of the Grand Master
The Street of the Knights
The hilltop Acropolis of Lindos, down the coast
rhodes_greece.tip: The walled old town is right by the port and free to wander for hours. Lindos and its clifftop acropolis are the big excursion — beautiful, but check the steep donkey-path climb suits you.

izmir.blurb: Izmir is Turkey's relaxed Aegean city and the gateway to Ephesus — one of the greatest classical sites in the world, its marble library and vast theatre astonishingly preserved. The city's seafront Kordon and bazaar add local colour.
miss: Ephesus's Library of Celsus and Great Theatre
The House of the Virgin Mary nearby
Izmir's Kemeraltı bazaar and Kordon promenade
izmir.tip: Ephesus is about an hour away and unmissable — go early to beat both heat and crowds. Combine it with the Terrace Houses (extra ticket) for the best-preserved Roman interiors.

kusadasi_ephesus_turkey.blurb: Kuşadası is the purpose-built gateway to Ephesus, the spectacular ruined Roman metropolis 20 minutes inland. The resort town itself has a bazaar, a causeway castle and plenty of carpet shops eager for your custom.
miss: Ephesus and the Library of Celsus
The Terrace Houses' Roman mosaics
The House of the Virgin Mary in the hills above
kusadasi_ephesus_turkey.tip: Ephesus is very close here, so you can go early and still have time for the town. Expect persistent but good-natured sales pressure in Kuşadası's bazaar — haggling is part of the game.

bodrum_turkey.blurb: Bodrum is Turkey's stylish Aegean resort, built around a Crusader castle that guards twin bays. The site of the ancient Mausoleum — one of the Seven Wonders — and a lively marina and bazaar give it substance beyond the beaches.
miss: Bodrum Castle and its Museum of Underwater Archaeology
The site of the Mausoleum at Halicarnassus
The marina, bazaar and whitewashed old town
bodrum_turkey.tip: The castle and its shipwreck museum are the standout and right in town — an easy walk from the harbour. The town is very walkable, so you rarely need transport here.

istanbul_turkey.blurb: Istanbul straddles two continents and layers Byzantine and Ottoman glory — the Hagia Sophia, the Blue Mosque, Topkapı Palace and the labyrinthine Grand Bazaar, most clustered in walkable Sultanahmet. The new Galataport terminal is central.
miss: Hagia Sophia and the Blue Mosque facing each other
Topkapı Palace and its harem
The Grand Bazaar and Spice Bazaar
istanbul_turkey.tip: The Galataport terminal is walkable to Karaköy and a short tram from Sultanahmet's big sights. Dress modestly for the mosques (scarves for women, covered shoulders/knees) and check prayer-time closures.

limassol.blurb: Limassol is Cyprus's easygoing coastal city, with a restored old port and marina, a medieval castle where Richard the Lionheart married, and the ancient sites of Kourion and Aphrodite's legendary birthplace along the coast.
miss: The ancient theatre and mosaics of Kourion
Limassol Castle in the old town
Aphrodite's Rock (Petra tou Romiou) down the coast
limassol.tip: Kourion's clifftop Greco-Roman theatre is the island highlight near Limassol — pair it with Aphrodite's Rock on the same coastal road for an easy, scenic half-day.

valletta_malta.blurb: Valletta is a golden Baroque city built by the Knights of St John, packed onto a peninsula of honey-stone palaces and gardens above the Grand Harbour. St John's Co-Cathedral hides a Caravaggio; the harbour views are sublime.
miss: St John's Co-Cathedral and its Caravaggio
The Upper Barrakka Gardens' harbour panorama
The ancient silent city of Mdina nearby
valletta_malta.tip: A lift from the harbour whisks you up into the city, saving the climb. Time the Upper Barrakka Gardens for noon or 4pm to catch the saluting-battery cannon firing over the harbour.

funchal_madeira_portugal.blurb: Funchal, capital of subtropical Madeira, is a city of flowers, fortified wine and dramatic scenery — a cable car up to Monte, a hair-raising wicker toboggan ride back down, and levada walks through the green interior.
miss: The cable car to Monte and its tropical gardens
The Monte wicker toboggan run down the hill
A Madeira wine lodge tasting in the old town
funchal_madeira_portugal.tip: The classic combo is the cable car up to Monte, the gardens, then the wicker-sledge ride down — book nothing in advance, just do them in that order for the signature Funchal day.

lanzarote_arrecife_spain.blurb: Lanzarote is a volcanic moonscape shaped by the artist César Manrique, whose vision defines the island — the fire mountains of Timanfaya, a cactus garden, and a house built inside lava bubbles. Unlike anywhere else in the Canaries.
miss: Timanfaya's fire mountains and geothermal demos
César Manrique's Jameos del Agua lava caves
The camel rides and volcanic vineyards of La Geria
lanzarote_arrecife_spain.tip: Timanfaya is the island's wonder but is only accessible by the official coach tour inside the park — a ship excursion or organised trip is the practical way to see it.

las_palmas_gran_canaria.blurb: Las Palmas mixes a historic quarter where Columbus once prayed with one of Europe's best city beaches, the golden Playa de las Canteras. The dramatic dunes and mountainous interior of Gran Canaria lie beyond.
miss: Playa de las Canteras city beach
The Vegueta old town and Columbus House
The Maspalomas dunes in the island's south
las_palmas_gran_canaria.tip: Canteras beach is a short ride or walk from the port and genuinely excellent — a rare port where a proper beach day is easy. The old Vegueta quarter is the cultural counterweight.

santa_cruz_de_tenerife_spain.blurb: Santa Cruz de Tenerife sits beneath Spain's highest peak, the volcanic Mount Teide, whose cable car climbs toward a 3,700m summit through a Martian national park. The wavy Auditorio and old La Laguna add culture.
miss: Mount Teide's cable car and volcanic park
The historic UNESCO town of La Laguna
The Calatrava-designed Auditorio de Tenerife
santa_cruz_de_tenerife_spain.tip: Teide is the star but a long ascent — a tour is the easy way, and the cable car needs decent weather to be worth it. La Laguna is a lovely, cooler alternative closer to port.

agadir_morocco.blurb: Agadir is Morocco's modern beach resort, rebuilt after a 1960 earthquake — a long sandy bay, a hilltop kasbah ruin with the city's name spelled in the hillside, and a souk that offers a gentler taste of Morocco than the imperial cities.
miss: The clifftop Kasbah ruins and hillside inscription
Souk El Had, one of Morocco's largest markets
The beach promenade and camel rides
agadir_morocco.tip: Agadir is more resort than deep-Morocco; for atmosphere, the walled town of Taroudant or the Paradise Valley oasis inland make richer excursions than the beach strip.

kahului_maui_hawaii.blurb: Kahului is the gateway to Maui, the Valley Isle — the switchbacking Road to Hana, the dormant Haleakalā volcano crater, and the old whaling town of Lahaina (still recovering from the 2023 fire). The port itself is industrial; Maui's beauty is spread out.
miss: The sunrise or summit view from Haleakalā crater
The Road to Hana's waterfalls and coast
Snorkelling at Molokini crater by boat
kahului_maui_hawaii.tip: Maui's highlights are far apart and a hire car or tour is essential — pick one big thing (Haleakalā OR Hana, not both) as each easily fills a port day.

kona_hawaii.blurb: Kona, on the Big Island's sunny Kona coast, is where the world's only US-grown coffee is farmed and where manta rays glide at night. The tenders land in walkable Kailua-Kona; volcanoes and coffee country lie beyond.
miss: A Kona coffee farm tour on the upland slopes
Snorkelling at Kealakekua Bay (Captain Cook monument)
Hawaii Volcanoes National Park, a longer drive
kona_hawaii.tip: It's a tender port and the town is compact and walkable — good for coffee and snorkelling close by. Volcanoes National Park is doable but a committed 2-hour-each-way drive.

hilo_hawaii.blurb: Hilo is the Big Island's lush, rainy eastern town — waterfalls, tropical gardens and the closest port to Hawaii Volcanoes National Park, where you can walk among steaming craters and hardened lava fields.
miss: Hawaii Volcanoes National Park's craters and lava tubes
Rainbow Falls just outside town
Akaka Falls' 400-foot plunge through rainforest
hilo_hawaii.tip: Volcanoes National Park is only about 45 minutes from Hilo — closer than from Kona — so this is the port to prioritise it. Bring a light rain layer; Hilo earns its green.

nawiliwili_kauai_hawaii.blurb: Nawiliwili is the port for Kauai, the Garden Isle — the oldest and most dramatically green of the main Hawaiian islands. Waimea Canyon, the sea cliffs of the Nā Pali Coast and Wailua's waterfalls are the draws.
miss: Waimea Canyon, the 'Grand Canyon of the Pacific'
The Nā Pali Coast by boat or helicopter
Fern Grotto and Wailua Falls
nawiliwili_kauai_hawaii.tip: Kauai's scenery is its whole point and needs transport — a boat trip along the roadless Nā Pali Coast is the single most spectacular way to spend the day here.

philadelphia.blurb: Philadelphia is where the United States was founded — Independence Hall and the Liberty Bell sit in a walkable historic core, alongside cobbled colonial streets and the art-museum steps Rocky made famous.
miss: Independence Hall and the Liberty Bell
The 'Rocky Steps' at the Philadelphia Museum of Art
Reading Terminal Market and a real cheesesteak
philadelphia.tip: Independence Hall entry is by timed ticket (free but limited) — reserve online ahead of a port call. The historic sites cluster tightly, so once there it's all on foot.

sydney_nova_scotia_canada.blurb: Sydney, on Cape Breton Island, greets ships with the world's largest fiddle on its boardwalk — a nod to the island's Celtic music heritage. It's the gateway to the spectacular Cabot Trail and the Fortress of Louisbourg.
miss: The Cabot Trail's coastal mountain drive
The reconstructed Fortress of Louisbourg
The giant fiddle and waterfront boardwalk
sydney_nova_scotia_canada.tip: The Cabot Trail is Cape Breton's glory but a long loop — for a shorter day, the Fortress of Louisbourg's costumed 18th-century world is closer and superb, especially in fall colour.

buenos_aires_argentina.blurb: Buenos Aires is the passionate, European-flavoured capital of Argentina — the birthplace of tango, the colourful houses of La Boca, the pink presidential palace on Plaza de Mayo, and steak and Malbec by the plateful.
miss: The colourful Caminito street in La Boca
Recoleta Cemetery, where Evita rests
A tango show, and an asado steak dinner
buenos_aires_argentina.tip: Many cruises overnight here — if yours does, an evening tango show is the essential Buenos Aires experience. The districts are spread out, so use taxis or apps between them.

montevideo_uruguay.blurb: Montevideo, Uruguay's low-key capital, pairs a walkable colonial Ciudad Vieja with a long riverside rambla promenade. The Mercado del Puerto's grills and the leafy Plaza Independencia are easy pleasures near the port.
miss: The Mercado del Puerto's parrilla grills
Ciudad Vieja's old town and Plaza Independencia
The rambla waterfront promenade
montevideo_uruguay.tip: The old town and the Mercado del Puerto (go hungry — it's a hall of wood-fired grills) are a short walk from the pier, making Montevideo an easy, low-stress port on foot.

punta_del_este_uruguay.blurb: Punta del Este is South America's glamorous beach resort — a peninsula of marinas and high-rises where the River Plate meets the Atlantic, best known for the giant sculpted fingers (La Mano) rising from the sand.
miss: La Mano — the giant hand emerging from the beach
The yacht harbour and Brava/Mansa beaches
Casapueblo, the artist Vilaró's white cliff villa
punta_del_este_uruguay.tip: It's a tender port and a compact, walkable resort — La Mano and the beaches are close. Casapueblo, along the coast, is a striking side-trip if you have time and a taxi.

puerto_madryn_argentina.blurb: Puerto Madryn is the gateway to Patagonia's wildlife — the Valdés Peninsula, where southern right whales, elephant seals, sea lions and Magellanic penguins gather depending on the season. A Welsh-settled heritage adds a curious twist.
miss: Whale watching off Península Valdés (season permitting)
The Punta Tombo penguin colony
Welsh tea houses in nearby Gaiman
puerto_madryn_argentina.tip: Wildlife here is highly seasonal — whales roughly June–Dec, penguins Sep–Mar. Check what's around for your date and book the matching excursion, as the sites are far from town.

ushuaia_argentina.blurb: Ushuaia bills itself as the End of the World — the southernmost city on earth, ringed by snow-topped peaks where the Andes meet the sea. It's the launch point for Antarctica and for the Tierra del Fuego National Park.
miss: Tierra del Fuego National Park's trails
A catamaran on the Beagle Channel to the lighthouse
The End of the World train and prison museum
ushuaia_argentina.tip: The Beagle Channel boat trip past sea-lion islands to Les Éclaireurs lighthouse is the classic Ushuaia excursion — and being this far south, weather turns fast, so layer up whatever the forecast says.

punta_arenas_chile.blurb: Punta Arenas sits on the Strait of Magellan, a windswept Patagonian city of grand wool-boom mansions and penguin colonies offshore. It's a gateway to Torres del Paine's peaks, though those are a long haul inland.
miss: The Magdalena Island penguin colony by boat
Plaza Muñoz Gamero's mansions and Magellan statue
The Nao Victoria museum's replica ships
punta_arenas_chile.tip: Magdalena Island's thousands of Magellanic penguins are the standout (Nov–Mar) but weather-dependent — the strait is notoriously windy, so sailings cancel; have a town plan as backup.

puerto_montt_chile.blurb: Puerto Montt is the gateway to Chile's Lake District — snow-capped volcanoes mirrored in deep blue lakes, the German-settled town of Puerto Varas, and the emerald Petrohué falls beneath Osorno volcano.
miss: Puerto Varas and Lake Llanquihue's volcano views
The Petrohué waterfalls below Osorno volcano
The Angelmó fishing market and its seafood
puerto_montt_chile.tip: The Lake District scenery around Puerto Varas is the reason to be here — a short drive from the port and far prettier than Puerto Montt itself, which is a working town.

valparaiso_chile.blurb: Valparaíso is Chile's bohemian port city, a UNESCO tangle of steep hills laced with Victorian funiculars and covered in vivid street art. Poet Pablo Neruda's quirky house looks out over the bay. Santiago lies inland.
miss: The hillside funiculars (ascensores) and street murals
Pablo Neruda's house, La Sebastiana
The Chilean wine valleys en route to Santiago
valparaiso_chile.tip: Valparaíso is best simply wandered — ride a creaking funicular up Cerro Concepción and follow the murals. Many cruises use it as the port for Santiago (90 min) or wine-country tours.

san_antonio_chile.blurb: San Antonio is Chile's busiest commercial port and an alternative gateway to Santiago and the Casablanca wine valley. The port town is workaday; the vineyards and the capital inland are where cruisers head.
miss: A Casablanca Valley winery tour
Santiago's old town and Sky Costanera view
Isla Negra, another Neruda house, up the coast
san_antonio_chile.tip: There's little to detain you in San Antonio itself — book a winery tour or the Santiago transfer, as the day's value is entirely inland.

callao_lima_peru.blurb: Callao is the port for Lima, Peru's sprawling capital — a UNESCO colonial centre of balconied mansions and catacombs, the clifftop Miraflores district above the Pacific, and a celebrated food scene that's among the world's best.
miss: Lima's colonial Plaza Mayor and San Francisco catacombs
The clifftop parks of Miraflores
Ceviche and a pisco sour in the culinary capital
callao_lima_peru.tip: Lima's traffic is heavy and Callao's immediate surrounds aren't for wandering — arrange transport to Miraflores or the historic centre, and don't leave Peru without proper ceviche.

manta_ecuador.blurb: Manta is Ecuador's tuna-fishing hub and the gateway to Panama-hat country — the hats are actually made here, not in Panama — plus cloud-forest coffee and the beach town of Montañita along the coast.
miss: A Panama-hat (Montecristi) weaving workshop
The beaches at nearby San Lorenzo
Coffee and cacao farm tours inland
manta_ecuador.tip: The 'Panama' hat is genuinely Ecuadorian and Montecristi nearby makes the finest — a hat-weaving visit is the characterful choice over the modest port town.

stanley_falklands.blurb: Stanley is the tiny, defiantly British capital of the Falklands — red phone boxes, a whalebone arch and a green-roofed cathedral at the far end of the South Atlantic. Penguin colonies on the outlying beaches are the real draw.
miss: King penguins at Volunteer Point (rough drive)
Gypsy Cove's Magellanic penguins, closer to town
Christ Church Cathedral and the whalebone arch
stanley_falklands.tip: This is a weather-and-swell-dependent tender port that's often missed entirely — if you get ashore, the penguins are the priority, but book early as 4x4 capacity to the colonies is very limited.

tokyo.blurb: Tokyo is a metropolis of dazzling contrasts — ancient Senso-ji temple and neon Shibuya, imperial gardens and robot-served cafés. Ships dock at various terminals; the city's superb trains connect everything for those willing to dive in.
miss: Senso-ji temple and Nakamise market in Asakusa
The Shibuya Crossing scramble
Meiji Shrine's forested calm and the imperial gardens
tokyo.tip: Depending on your ship you'll dock at Yokohama, Tokyo (Harumi/Aomi) or elsewhere — check which, as the commute into central Tokyo varies from 20 minutes to over an hour. Get a prepaid Suica card for the trains.

yokohama.blurb: Yokohama is Tokyo's cosmopolitan port neighbour, with the striking Minato Mirai waterfront, Japan's largest Chinatown, and the atmospheric Sankeien garden. It's also a common berth for cruises 'to Tokyo', 30 minutes up the line.
miss: The Minato Mirai waterfront and Cup Noodles Museum
Yokohama Chinatown, Japan's biggest
Sankeien traditional garden
yokohama.tip: You can have a full day in Yokohama itself without the Tokyo trek — the waterfront, Chinatown and garden are close together and less frantic than the capital.

kobe.blurb: Kobe is a stylish port city famous worldwide for its marbled beef, framed by mountains and bay. It's also the gateway to the ancient capitals of Kyoto and Nara, each under an hour away by fast train.
miss: Kyoto's temples and geisha district (an hour away)
Nara's bowing deer and Great Buddha
Kobe beef, and the Nunobiki herb-garden ropeway
kobe.tip: Kyoto tempts everyone from Kobe, but it's a full day and you'll only scratch it — pick two or three Kyoto sights, or stay local for Kobe beef and the harbour, which is lovely in its own right.

nagasaki.blurb: Nagasaki is a hilly, harbour-wrapped city with a unique history — Japan's window to the West for centuries, and the second atomic-bomb target. The moving Peace Park and the Dutch-era Dejima trading post tell its story.
miss: The Atomic Bomb Museum and Peace Park
Dejima, the reconstructed Dutch trading island
The Glover Garden and harbour night view
nagasaki.tip: The Peace Park and museum are essential and sobering — allow real time and headspace for them. The city's efficient trams reach every major site cheaply.

fukuoka.blurb: Fukuoka is Kyushu's laid-back largest city, famous for its tonkotsu ramen and its riverside yatai food stalls at night. Ancient Dazaifu shrine and a ruined castle's cherry-blossom park give it depth beyond the food.
miss: Dazaifu Tenmangu shrine and its plum trees
The yatai street-food stalls (if you overnight)
Ohori Park and the castle ruins
fukuoka.tip: Fukuoka is Japan's ramen capital — a bowl of Hakata tonkotsu is the local rite. Dazaifu shrine is a short train ride and the best half-day out.

sapporo-otaru.blurb: Otaru is a charming canal town of restored stone warehouses and glassworks, and the port for Sapporo, Hokkaido's beer-brewing capital 40 minutes inland. Northern Japan's seafood and dairy are exceptional here.
miss: Otaru's historic canal and glass workshops
Sapporo's beer museum and Odori Park
The freshest sushi and uni (sea urchin) in Japan
sapporo-otaru.tip: Otaru itself is a lovely walkable half-day of canal and craft shops — pair it with the train to Sapporo, or just feast on Hokkaido seafood without going anywhere.

busan.blurb: Busan is South Korea's beach-and-seafood second city — the tumbling pastel houses of Gamcheon Culture Village, the vast Jagalchi fish market, and a hilltop temple perched right over the sea.
miss: Gamcheon Culture Village's rainbow hillside
Haedong Yonggungsa, the seaside temple
Jagalchi fish market and Haeundae Beach
busan.tip: Gamcheon is Busan's photo-star but gets busy — go early. The metro is easy and English-signed, making independent exploring straightforward here.

incheon-seoul.blurb: Incheon is the port for Seoul, South Korea's vast, hyper-modern capital an hour inland — grand royal palaces beside K-pop and skyscrapers, buzzing markets and the DMZ border with the North within reach.
miss: Gyeongbokgung Palace and its guard-changing
Bukchon Hanok village's traditional houses
A DMZ tour to the Korean border
incheon-seoul.tip: Seoul is a long hour from Incheon and huge — pick a focused area (the palace-and-hanok historic core is best) rather than trying to criss-cross the city on a port day.

jeju.blurb: Jeju is South Korea's volcanic holiday island — a UNESCO landscape of a sunrise-crater peak, lava-tube caves, waterfalls and the famous haenyeo women free-divers who harvest the sea by hand.
miss: Seongsan Ilchulbong, the sunrise crater peak
Manjanggul lava tube cave
A haenyeo diving demonstration
jeju.tip: Jeju's sights ring the island and need transport — the sunrise peak and a lava tube make a natural pairing on the east side, close enough to combine in one day.

shanghai.blurb: Shanghai is China's dazzling showcase — the colonial Bund facing the space-age Pudong skyline across the river, the classical Yu Garden, and a metro that makes the megacity navigable. Note: ships often dock far from the centre.
miss: The Bund waterfront and Pudong skyline
Yu Garden and the old city bazaar
A fast Maglev train ride from the airport
shanghai.tip: China requires visas, though many cruise passengers use a visa-free group-tour arrangement — check your line's rules carefully before the call. The cruise terminal at Baoshan is a long way out; factor in the transfer.

beijing-tianjin.blurb: Tianjin is the port for Beijing — but the capital and its Great Wall are a demanding 2.5–3 hours inland. This is very much a book-the-excursion call, delivering the Forbidden City, Tiananmen Square and the Wall itself.
miss: The Great Wall at Mutianyu or Badaling
The Forbidden City and Tiananmen Square
The Temple of Heaven's park
beijing-tianjin.tip: Beijing is a long haul and usually needs an overnight to do properly — on a single port day, a ship excursion is strongly advised given the distance, visa logistics and traffic. Tianjin itself has a pleasant European-style riverfront if you stay.

dalian.blurb: Dalian is a breezy northern Chinese port with a Russian and Japanese colonial past, leafy squares, tram lines and a dramatic coastal drive. It's more relaxed and walkable than China's mega-cities.
miss: Zhongshan Square's colonial architecture
The coastal Binhai Road drive
Xinghai Square, one of the world's largest
dalian.tip: Dalian is one of China's more pleasant, low-key ports — the old trams and seafront squares are easy to enjoy, though China visa rules still apply, so check before independent exploring.

xiamen.blurb: Xiamen is a subtropical southern-Chinese city whose jewel is Gulangyu — a car-free UNESCO island of colonial villas and piano music, a short ferry from the port. Tea culture and temples round out the call.
miss: Gulangyu island's colonial mansions (car-free)
Nanputuo Temple below Wulao peak
Xiamen's café-lined old streets
xiamen.tip: Gulangyu is the highlight and ferry-access only, with visitor caps — a ship tour handles the ticketing, which can otherwise be fiddly independently. China visa rules apply.

shenzhen.blurb: Shenzhen rose from a fishing village to a tech megacity in a generation, sitting right on the Hong Kong border. It's modern China at full throttle — vast malls, theme parks and the electronics markets of Huaqiangbei.
miss: The Huaqiangbei electronics markets
Window of the World's global-landmark park
The OCT-LOFT arts district
shenzhen.tip: Shenzhen is more a working megacity than a classic sightseeing port — its border with Hong Kong makes it a useful gateway, but check visa arrangements, which differ from the rest of China here.

hong-kong.blurb: Hong Kong is one of the world's great harbour cities — the Peak tram to a skyline panorama, the Star Ferry across Victoria Harbour, dim sum, temples and neon markets, all knitted by a superb transit system.
miss: The Peak Tram and the skyline view
The Star Ferry across Victoria Harbour
Dim sum, and the Big Buddha on Lantau
hong-kong.tip: Hong Kong is wonderfully easy and English-friendly — get an Octopus card and explore independently. The Symphony of Lights harbour show at 8pm is worth staying aboard-late for if timings allow.

keelung-taipei.blurb: Keelung is the rainy northern port for Taipei, Taiwan's vibrant capital 40 minutes away — the soaring Taipei 101 tower, the treasure-filled National Palace Museum, and the mountain teahouses of Jiufen nearby.
miss: Taipei 101's observation deck
The National Palace Museum's Chinese treasures
Jiufen's lantern-lit mountain lanes
keelung-taipei.tip: Jiufen (the town said to inspire Spirited Away) is closer to Keelung than central Taipei and utterly atmospheric — a great alternative to the city rush, especially in the late afternoon.

kaohsiung.blurb: Kaohsiung is Taiwan's sunny southern harbour city — the ornate Dragon and Tiger Pagodas on Lotus Lake, a lively night-market scene, and the arts-district warehouses of Pier-2 right by the port.
miss: The Dragon and Tiger Pagodas at Lotus Pond
The Pier-2 Art Center by the harbour
Liuhe or Ruifeng night market
kaohsiung.tip: Kaohsiung is flatter, sunnier and more relaxed than Taipei, with an easy metro — the Pier-2 arts area is walkable from the terminal for a low-effort start.

ho-chi-minh-city.blurb: Ships dock at Phu My, about two hours from Ho Chi Minh City (Saigon) — a city of French-colonial landmarks, the sobering War Remnants Museum, the frantic Ben Thanh market and the Cu Chi tunnels outside town.
miss: The War Remnants Museum and Reunification Palace
The Cu Chi tunnels network
Ben Thanh market and Notre-Dame Saigon
ho-chi-minh-city.tip: The 2-hour transfer each way makes this an all-day excursion, not an independent wander — a ship or private tour is the practical choice given the distance from Phu My.

vung-tau.blurb: Vung Tau is a Vietnamese beach town on a headland, once a French colonial resort, now watched over by a giant Christ statue with arms outstretched over the sea — climbable for a sweeping coastal view. It's also an alternative gateway to Saigon.
miss: The Christ of Vung Tau statue climb (811 steps)
The Lighthouse and Back Beach seafront
The White Palace colonial villa
vung-tau.tip: Vung Tau is far closer to its seafront sights than Phu My is to Saigon — if your ship calls here, a relaxed local day of statue, beach and seafood beats the long city haul.

da-nang.blurb: Da Nang is central Vietnam's beach city and gateway to two wonders — the ancient lantern-lit town of Hoi An, and the mountain-top Golden Bridge held aloft by giant stone hands at the Ba Na Hills.
miss: Hoi An's UNESCO old town and lanterns
The Golden Bridge and Ba Na Hills
The Marble Mountains' caves and shrines
da-nang.tip: Hoi An (about 45 minutes) is the standout and glorious after dark when the lanterns glow — if your call runs late, save it for then; otherwise go early before the heat and crowds.

hue-chan-may.walk: Coach to Hue or Hoi An
hue-chan-may.blurb: Chan May is the port serving both the old imperial capital of Hue — its walled Citadel and riverside royal tombs — and, in the other direction, Hoi An and Da Nang. A crossroads call with two very different day options.
miss: Hue's Imperial Citadel and Forbidden City
The Thien Mu Pagoda on the Perfume River
Or south to Hoi An's lantern-lit old town
hue-chan-may.tip: Decide in advance: north to imperial Hue (about 90 min) or south to Hoi An (about 90 min) — they're opposite directions, so you realistically get one, not both.

cam-ranh.blurb: Cam Ranh is the deep-water port for Nha Trang, Vietnam's premier beach resort — a long golden bay, Cham-era Hindu towers, and island-hopping boat trips in clear water. Mud baths are a local specialty.
miss: Nha Trang's beach and Po Nagar Cham towers
An island-hopping snorkel boat trip
A therapeutic mineral mud bath
cam-ranh.tip: Nha Trang is about 45 minutes from the port — an easy beach-and-culture day. The Cham towers plus a beach afternoon is the classic, relaxed pairing here.

dalat-nha-trang.blurb: This call serves both the coastal resort of Nha Trang and, for the adventurous, the cool highland town of Dalat — a French hill-station of flower gardens, waterfalls and pine forest high above the coast.
miss: Nha Trang's beaches and Cham towers
Dalat's French-era villas and flower gardens (long drive)
The Ponagar towers and a mud-bath spa
dalat-nha-trang.tip: Dalat is a spectacular but long mountain drive (3+ hours) — for a port day, Nha Trang's coast is far more realistic; leave Dalat for a longer stay.

qui-nhon.blurb: Qui Nhon is an up-and-coming central-Vietnam beach city, still refreshingly untouristed — a sweeping crescent bay, ancient Cham towers on the hills, and the Thap Doi twin towers right in town.
miss: The Banh It and Thap Doi Cham towers
Ky Co beach and Eo Gio's rugged cliffs
The quiet city beachfront promenade
qui-nhon.tip: Qui Nhon rewards a relaxed approach — it lacks a single blockbuster sight, so enjoy the beaches and Cham ruins at an easy pace rather than rushing.

phu-quoc.blurb: Phu Quoc is Vietnam's tropical island escape in the Gulf of Thailand — white-sand beaches, a fish-sauce heritage, pepper farms, and the world's longest over-sea cable car to the An Thoi islands.
miss: The Hon Thom cable car over the sea
Sao Beach's powder-white sand
A fish-sauce or pepper-farm visit
phu-quoc.tip: It's a tender port and an island of beaches — the record-breaking cable car is the signature experience, but a simple beach day is a perfectly good use of Phu Quoc.

hai-phong.blurb: Hai Phong is northern Vietnam's port city — a gateway to the limestone wonder of Halong Bay and, further off, to Hanoi. The city itself has faded French-colonial grandeur and flame-tree-lined boulevards.
miss: A cruise among Halong Bay's limestone karsts
Cat Ba Island's national park
Hai Phong's colonial opera house and old quarter
hai-phong.tip: Halong Bay is the reason most ships call here — a junk-boat cruise among the karsts is unmissable, but it's a fair drive, so book the excursion rather than improvising.

halong-bay.blurb: Halong Bay is the show itself — thousands of jungle-topped limestone islands rising from emerald water, a UNESCO seascape best seen from a traditional junk gliding between the karsts and into hidden caves.
miss: A junk cruise among the limestone islands
Sung Sot (Surprise) Cave's vast chambers
A kayak into a quiet lagoon
halong-bay.tip: The bay is all about getting on the water — a boat trip among the karsts is the entire point, so prioritise that over anything on land.

hanoi-halong-bay.blurb: This call pairs Halong Bay with Vietnam's capital Hanoi — a city of a chaotic Old Quarter, the serene Hoan Kiem Lake, Ho Chi Minh's mausoleum and the Temple of Literature. Hanoi is a long haul from the coast, though.
miss: Hanoi's Old Quarter and Hoan Kiem Lake
The Temple of Literature
Or a Halong Bay junk cruise instead
hanoi-halong-bay.tip: Hanoi is 2.5+ hours from the Halong coast — you realistically choose the city OR the bay for a port day, not both. The bay is the easier, closer, more relaxing option.

phnom-penh.blurb: Some river-capable ships reach Phnom Penh itself, Cambodia's riverside capital — the golden Royal Palace and Silver Pagoda, bustling markets, and the deeply moving sites of the Khmer Rouge era.
miss: The Royal Palace and Silver Pagoda
The National Museum's Khmer sculpture
The Tuol Sleng and Killing Fields memorials
phnom-penh.tip: The genocide memorial sites are harrowing but important — approach them with time and respect. The riverfront and palace offer a gentler counterbalance for the rest of the day.

sihanoukville.blurb: Sihanoukville is Cambodia's coastal gateway — once a sleepy beach town, now rapidly developed, but still the jumping-off point for tropical islands, fishing villages and the Ream National Park's mangroves and wildlife.
miss: A boat trip to Koh Rong's beaches
Ream National Park's mangroves
The hilltop Wat Leu pagoda and its views
sihanoukville.tip: The town has changed fast and unevenly — the offshore islands and the national park are the appealing escapes, so head for the water rather than staying in the port.

bangkok-laem-chabang.blurb: Laem Chabang is the port for Bangkok — a glittering, chaotic capital of golden temples, the Grand Palace, floating markets and street food, about two hours inland. Pattaya's beaches lie much closer to the pier.
miss: The Grand Palace and Wat Pho's reclining Buddha
A longtail boat through the canals (khlongs)
Or nearer Pattaya's beaches and Sanctuary of Truth
bangkok-laem-chabang.tip: Bangkok is a committed 2-hour transfer each way — thrilling but a long day. If temples-and-traffic feels much, Pattaya is 30 minutes away for an easier beach-and-culture call.

hua-hin.blurb: Hua Hin is Thailand's original royal beach resort, more refined and relaxed than Pattaya — a long sandy beach, a quirky railway station, night markets, and the summer palace of the Thai kings.
miss: The historic Hua Hin railway station
The night market's seafood and stalls
Cicada weekend arts market, if timing suits
hua-hin.tip: Hua Hin is a genuinely pleasant, low-key resort — walkable and unhurried, with the beach and night market the easy pleasures. No blockbuster sights, just a relaxed Thai day.

koh-samui.blurb: Koh Samui is Thailand's palm-fringed Gulf island — the Big Buddha on its islet, coconut groves, waterfalls, and the powdery sands of Chaweng and Lamai. A laid-back tropical day awaits ashore.
miss: The Big Buddha temple (Wat Phra Yai)
Chaweng or Lamai beach
Na Muang waterfalls in the jungle interior
koh-samui.tip: It's a tender port and an island of beaches and temples — the Big Buddha plus a beach afternoon is the classic combination, all within an easy taxi ride.

koh-tao.blurb: Koh Tao — 'Turtle Island' — is a tiny Gulf-of-Thailand gem renowned as one of the world's best and cheapest places to snorkel and dive, with clear water, coral gardens and, true to its name, sea turtles.
miss: Snorkelling or diving at Shark Bay or Mango Bay
The viewpoint over Koh Nang Yuan's sandbar
A longtail boat around the coves
koh-tao.tip: The underwater life is the whole point here — bring or rent a snorkel and get in the water. The Koh Nang Yuan viewpoint over the triple-island sandbar is the iconic photo.

koh-phi-phi.blurb: Koh Phi Phi is the Andaman Sea's postcard — sheer limestone cliffs, turquoise bays and the famous Maya Bay of 'The Beach'. Stunning but popular; boat trips reveal its lagoons and snorkelling spots.
miss: Maya Bay (now with visitor limits)
Snorkelling at Pileh Lagoon
The Phi Phi viewpoint hike
koh-phi-phi.tip: Maya Bay has strict caps and sometimes closes to protect the reef — check status, and expect company; going by boat early beats the midday flotilla.

phuket.blurb: Phuket is Thailand's largest island and Andaman hub — Patong's buzzing beach and nightlife, the giant hilltop Big Buddha, the old Sino-Portuguese town, and boat trips to the karst-studded Phang Nga Bay.
miss: Phang Nga Bay and James Bond Island by boat
The Big Buddha viewpoint
Phuket Old Town's colourful shophouses
phuket.tip: Phang Nga Bay's limestone scenery is the standout excursion. Phuket Old Town is an underrated, walkable alternative to the beaches if you'd rather culture than sand.

krabi.blurb: Krabi is the mainland gateway to the Andaman's most dramatic scenery — the towering limestone cliffs of Railay, the Emerald Pool and hot springs in the jungle, and boat trips to the Hong Islands' hidden lagoons.
miss: Railay's cliff-backed beaches by longtail
The Emerald Pool and jungle hot springs
A Hong Islands island-hopping trip
krabi.tip: Railay is only reachable by boat and worth it for the cliff scenery — a longtail from the pier is part of the fun. The Emerald Pool is the pick for an inland, jungle-cooled day.

khao-lak.blurb: Khao Lak is a quieter stretch of Andaman coast north of Phuket, backed by rainforest national parks — a launch point for the pristine Similan Islands' diving and for elephant sanctuaries inland.
miss: The Similan Islands' reefs (seasonal)
An ethical elephant sanctuary visit
Khao Lak's long, calm beaches
khao-lak.tip: The Similan Islands are among the world's top dive/snorkel sites but only open roughly Oct–May — check the season. Choose reputable, hands-off elephant sanctuaries over riding camps.

koh-lanta.blurb: Koh Lanta is a mellow, long-beached Andaman island that has kept its low-key charm — old-town stilt houses, mangrove kayaking, and a national park with a lighthouse at the island's quiet southern tip.
miss: Lanta Old Town's wooden stilt shophouses
Mu Ko Lanta National Park and lighthouse
A mangrove or four-islands boat trip
koh-lanta.tip: Koh Lanta is about slowing down — it has no must-see monuments, so lean into the beaches, the old town and the mangroves at island pace.

trat-koh-chang.blurb: Trat is the mainland province serving Koh Chang and its archipelago near the Cambodian border — a region of jungle-covered islands, quiet beaches and mangrove estuaries in Thailand's far southeast.
miss: A boat to Koh Chang's beaches and waterfalls
Mangrove kayaking in the estuaries
The old teak-shophouse Trat town market
trat-koh-chang.tip: This is off the mainstream trail — the appeal is quiet islands and mangroves, so take to the water rather than expecting big-name sights.

chiang-mai.blurb: Chiang Mai is northern Thailand's cultural capital — moated old city temples, mountain-top Doi Suthep, night bazaars and ethical elephant sanctuaries. It sits deep inland, so it features as an overland tour or fly-in, not a walk-off port.
miss: Wat Phra That Doi Suthep on its mountain
The old city's ancient temples
An ethical elephant sanctuary in the hills
chiang-mai.tip: Chiang Mai is far from any coast — if it appears on your itinerary it'll be a multi-day overland or fly-in add-on, not a typical port day. Plan it as a tour, not an independent visit.

singapore.blurb: Singapore is a gleaming, green, hyper-efficient city-state — the Marina Bay Sands skyline, the futuristic Gardens by the Bay Supertrees, hawker-centre feasts and colonial and ethnic quarters, all linked by a flawless metro.
miss: Gardens by the Bay and the Supertree Grove
Marina Bay and the Merlion
A hawker-centre meal (Michelin street food)
singapore.tip: Singapore is the easiest port in Asia to do independently — impeccable English, superb MRT metro, total safety. It's also a common turnaround port, so allow an extra day if you can; there's plenty to fill it.

port-klang-kuala-lumpur.blurb: Port Klang is the gateway to Kuala Lumpur, an hour inland — the soaring Petronas Twin Towers, the cave temples of Batu with their giant golden statue, colonial Merdeka Square and a superb street-food scene.
miss: The Petronas Twin Towers and skybridge
The Batu Caves' 272 rainbow steps
Merdeka Square and the food of Jalan Alor
port-klang-kuala-lumpur.tip: KL is about an hour from Port Klang — reachable by a cheap commuter train (KTM) as well as tours. The Batu Caves sit on the KL fringe and pair naturally with the city.

penang.blurb: Penang's George Town is a UNESCO feast — Chinese clan houses, colonial mansions, celebrated street art and some of Asia's best street food, all in a walkable old core right by the port. A genuine highlight.
miss: George Town's street art and clan jetties
Kek Lok Si, Malaysia's largest temple
Penang Hill's funicular and the food of Gurney Drive
penang.tip: George Town is made for wandering on foot straight off the ship — hunt the murals, duck into clan houses, and eat everything. It's one of Southeast Asia's most rewarding walk-off ports.

langkawi.blurb: Langkawi is Malaysia's duty-free tropical island — rainforest and mangroves, a vertiginous SkyCab cable car to a curved sky bridge, eagles wheeling over the sea, and quiet beaches. Nature and duty-free shopping in one.
miss: The SkyCab cable car and Sky Bridge
A mangrove-and-eagle-watching boat tour
Cenang Beach and duty-free shopping
langkawi.tip: The SkyCab up Gunung Mat Cincang is the star — go early before cloud builds over the summit. The mangrove tours are the other great option for wildlife.

kuala-terengganu.blurb: Kuala Terengganu, on Malaysia's traditional east coast, offers a more authentically Malay experience — the striking Crystal Mosque, a Chinatown of murals, songket weaving and boat trips to nearby islands.
miss: The Crystal Mosque at Islamic Heritage Park
Chinatown's street art and shophouses
The Central Market's crafts and songket
kuala-terengganu.tip: This is a conservative, traditional region — dress modestly, especially near mosques. It's a quieter, more local port than Malaysia's big cities, best taken at an easy pace.

jakarta.blurb: Jakarta is Indonesia's vast, traffic-snarled capital — the old Dutch Batavia quarter around Fatahillah Square, the giant National Monument, and the enormous Istiqlal Mosque facing a cathedral in a study of tolerance.
miss: Old Batavia (Kota Tua) and Fatahillah Square
The National Monument (Monas)
Istiqlal Mosque and the old harbour
jakarta.tip: Jakarta's traffic is legendary — keep plans compact and near the old town rather than criss-crossing the city, and build in generous time back to the ship.

surabaya.blurb: Surabaya is Indonesia's gritty second city, a place of independence-war history, a colourful Arab and Chinese quarter, and — for the ambitious — a distant gateway to the volcanic moonscape of Mount Bromo.
miss: The House of Sampoerna colonial tobacco museum
The Arab Quarter and Ampel Mosque
Submarine monument and the old town
surabaya.tip: Mount Bromo is spectacular but a pre-dawn, multi-hour expedition unsuited to a normal port day — for the call itself, the colonial and Arab quarters make the better focus.

bali-benoa.blurb: Benoa is Bali's cruise port — gateway to the Island of the Gods, with its clifftop sea temples, emerald rice terraces, monkey forests and Hindu culture. Ubud's arts scene and Uluwatu's temple are the classic day-trips.
miss: The clifftop Uluwatu sea temple and kecak dance
Ubud's rice terraces and monkey forest
Tanah Lot temple on its offshore rock
bali-benoa.tip: Bali's traffic is heavy and sights are spread out — pick one region (the Uluwatu/south cliffs, or Ubud's culture inland) rather than both. Dress respectfully for temple visits (a sarong is often required).

boracay-caticlan.blurb: Caticlan is the jump-off for Boracay, the Philippine island regularly voted among the world's best beaches — the powder-white four-kilometre sweep of White Beach, turquoise water and legendary sunsets.
miss: White Beach's powder sand and sunset
Island-hopping to Puka Shell Beach
Snorkelling the offshore reefs
boracay-caticlan.tip: Boracay reaches it via a short boat from Caticlan — it's all about the beach, so keep it simple: sand, swim, and a sunset if your timing allows.

manila.blurb: Manila is the Philippines' dense, energetic capital — the walled Spanish colonial core of Intramuros with Fort Santiago and the old cathedral, the seaside Rizal Park, and a warm, English-speaking welcome.
miss: Intramuros' walls, Fort Santiago and San Agustin church
Rizal Park and the old Manila Bay sunset
A calesa (horse carriage) ride through the old city
manila.tip: Intramuros is the walkable historic heart and best explored by bamboo bike or calesa — Manila's traffic is intense, so stay concentrated there rather than ranging across the city.

brunei-muara.blurb: Muara serves Bandar Seri Begawan, the capital of oil-rich Brunei — gold-domed mosques, the sprawling stilt-village of Kampong Ayer on the river, and a sultan's ceremonial splendour, all in a quiet, devout Islamic sultanate.
miss: The Sultan Omar Ali Saifuddien Mosque
Kampong Ayer, the water village, by boat
The Royal Regalia Museum's opulence
brunei-muara.tip: Brunei is conservative — dress modestly and note alcohol is banned. The stilt-village boat trip (with luck, a proboscis-monkey sighting) is the memorable local experience.

yangon.blurb: Yangon is Myanmar's largest city, where the colossal golden Shwedagon Pagoda dominates the skyline — one of Buddhism's most sacred sites. Faded British-colonial architecture and bustling markets fill the streets below.
miss: The Shwedagon Pagoda, especially at dusk
The Sule Pagoda and colonial downtown
Bogyoke Aung San (Scott) Market
yangon.tip: Shwedagon is extraordinary at sunset when the gold glows — if your call runs late, save it for then. Remove shoes and socks before entering, and dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered).

mumbai.blurb: Mumbai is India's pulsing commercial capital — the Gateway of India arch on the waterfront, Victorian-Gothic and Art Deco landmarks, the vast Dhobi Ghat laundry, and Bollywood glamour amid teeming energy.
miss: The Gateway of India and Taj Mahal Palace hotel
The UNESCO Victorian and Art Deco ensemble
Elephanta Island's cave temples by ferry
mumbai.tip: Mumbai is intense and sprawling — the colonial core around the Gateway is walkable and best for a port day. Elephanta's cave temples are a worthwhile ferry ride if the harbour's calm.

goa.blurb: Goa blends Indian and Portuguese worlds — palm-fringed beaches, the baroque churches of Old Goa (where St Francis Xavier lies), spice plantations, and a relaxed coastal pace unlike anywhere else in India.
miss: Old Goa's Basilica of Bom Jesus and cathedral
A spice-plantation tour and Goan lunch
The beaches of the north or south coast
goa.tip: Goa splits between beach and heritage — Old Goa's churches are a compact, fascinating morning, leaving the afternoon for a beach or spice farm. The Portuguese-Indian cuisine is a highlight in itself.

cochin-kochi.blurb: Kochi is the relaxed jewel of India's Kerala coast — the Chinese fishing nets of Fort Kochi, a 16th-century synagogue in Jew Town, spice markets, and backwater cruises through palm-lined lagoons.
miss: The cantilevered Chinese fishing nets at sunset
Mattancherry Palace and the Paradesi Synagogue
A Kerala backwater houseboat cruise
cochin-kochi.tip: Fort Kochi is walkable and atmospheric, with the fishing nets, synagogue and spice lanes close together. A backwater cruise is the other classic — quintessentially Keralan and wonderfully calm.

chennai.blurb: Chennai, capital of Tamil Nadu, is a gateway to South India's Dravidian temple culture — the shore temples of Mahabalipuram down the coast, a long city beach, British Fort St George, and a rich classical-arts heritage.
miss: Mahabalipuram's UNESCO shore temples
Marina Beach, one of the world's longest
Kapaleeshwarar Temple and Fort St George
chennai.tip: Mahabalipuram (about 90 minutes south) is the highlight — its carved monuments by the sea outshine the city's own sights, so it's worth the drive if temples interest you.

visakhapatnam.blurb: Visakhapatnam ('Vizag') is a scenic port on India's east coast, ringed by the Eastern Ghats hills — beaches, a submarine museum on the sand, ancient Buddhist ruins nearby, and a hill-station viewpoint above the bay.
miss: The INS Kursura submarine museum on the beach
Kailasagiri hilltop park and its views
The Buddhist ruins at Thotlakonda
visakhapatnam.tip: Vizag is a less-touristed Indian port with an appealing coast — the submarine museum and the hilltop park are easy, and the Buddhist sites reward those after something quieter.

colombo.blurb: Colombo, Sri Lanka's seaside capital, mixes colonial-era landmarks, a red-and-white striped mosque, buzzing Pettah bazaars and the breezy Galle Face Green — with the option of heading to elephants or ancient temples inland.
miss: The Gangaramaya Temple and Pettah markets
The colonial Fort district and Galle Face Green
The Jami Ul-Alfar (red) Mosque
colombo.tip: Colombo itself makes an easy walkable-by-tuk-tuk day. Longer trips to elephant orphanages or the hill country are possible but eat the whole day — decide based on your call length.

male.blurb: Malé is the compact, densely packed capital of the Maldives — a colourful island city of a coral-stone mosque, a fish market and tightly gridded streets — and the gateway to the turquoise lagoons and resort islands beyond.
miss: The Old Friday Mosque's carved coral stone
The fish and produce markets
A boat to a nearby sandbank or reef for snorkelling
male.tip: Malé itself is a quick walkable circuit; the Maldives' magic is the water, so a snorkelling or sandbank boat trip is the way to spend the day. Dress modestly in the conservative capital.

muscat.blurb: Muscat is Oman's refined, low-rise capital, wrapped by dramatic bare mountains — the vast Sultan Qaboos Grand Mosque, the lively Mutrah souq along the corniche, and forts guarding the old harbour. Arabia at its most elegant.
miss: The Sultan Qaboos Grand Mosque (morning entry)
The Mutrah Souq and corniche
The Royal Opera House and old-town forts
muscat.tip: The Grand Mosque only admits non-Muslim visitors in the morning (roughly 8–11am) and requires modest dress — women need to cover hair, arms and legs — so go early and prepared.

dubai.blurb: Dubai is superlatives made concrete — the world's tallest building the Burj Khalifa, the sail-shaped Burj Al Arab, vast malls with indoor ski slopes, and the old-world contrast of the Creek's spice and gold souks.
miss: The Burj Khalifa observation deck (pre-book)
The old Creek, abras and the gold and spice souks
A desert safari with dunes and dinner
dubai.tip: Many cruises overnight in Dubai — if yours does, a desert safari is the essential add-on. Burj Khalifa tickets are far cheaper and less queued when booked online in advance.

abu-dhabi.blurb: Abu Dhabi, the UAE capital, pairs the breathtaking white-marble Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque with the Louvre Abu Dhabi's domed museum and the Ferrari World theme park — a calmer, more spacious counterpart to Dubai.
miss: The Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque
Louvre Abu Dhabi under its floating dome
The Corniche, or Ferrari World for thrill-seekers
abu-dhabi.tip: The Grand Mosque is the unmissable sight and free to enter, but enforces strict modest dress (abayas can be borrowed) — go early to beat both heat and crowds.

doha.blurb: Doha, Qatar's fast-rising capital, balances tradition and spectacle — the restored Souq Waqif's alleys, the sail-inspired Museum of Islamic Art, the striking National Museum, and a futuristic skyline across the bay.
miss: Souq Waqif's restored alleys and falcon market
The Museum of Islamic Art and its park
The dune-and-sea 'inland sea' desert excursion
doha.tip: Souq Waqif is best in the cooler evening when it comes alive — if your call runs late, save it for then. The Museum of Islamic Art is a superb, air-conditioned midday refuge.

aqaba.blurb: Aqaba is Jordan's Red Sea port and the gateway to two wonders — the rose-red rock city of Petra, and the Martian desert of Wadi Rum. The town itself offers Red Sea snorkelling and a relaxed corniche.
miss: Petra, the rock-carved Nabataean city
Wadi Rum's desert landscapes by 4x4
Red Sea snorkelling on Aqaba's reefs
aqaba.tip: Petra is about two hours each way and a lot of walking — an unforgettable but full day, best booked as an organised excursion given the distance and the site's scale. Wear proper shoes.

haifa.blurb: Haifa cascades down Mount Carmel to the sea, crowned by the golden-domed Bahá'í Shrine and its immaculate terraced gardens. It's also the gateway to Nazareth, Acre's crusader city, and the Galilee.
miss: The Bahá'í Gardens' terraced hanging gardens
Crusader Acre (Akko) up the coast
Nazareth or the Sea of Galilee inland
haifa.tip: The Bahá'í Gardens are Haifa's signature and partly free to view, though the full terrace tour needs booking. Acre's atmospheric old city is the best nearby half-day.

ashdod.blurb: Ashdod is the port for Jerusalem and Bethlehem, about 90 minutes inland — among the most significant destinations in the world, holy to three faiths, with the Western Wall, the Church of the Holy Sepulchre and the Dome of the Rock.
miss: Jerusalem's Old City and the Western Wall
The Church of the Holy Sepulchre
Bethlehem's Church of the Nativity, or Tel Aviv's beaches
ashdod.tip: Jerusalem is the obvious draw but a long, full and emotionally weighty day — an organised tour handles the distance and the complex logistics best. Dress modestly for the religious sites.

sydney.blurb: Sydney needs little introduction — ships glide past the Opera House's white sails and the Harbour Bridge to berth at Circular Quay or White Bay. The harbour, the Rocks' colonial lanes, and Bondi Beach define the day.
miss: The Opera House and a Harbour Bridge climb or walk
The Rocks' historic lanes and markets
A ferry to Manly or Taronga Zoo across the harbour
sydney.tip: If you dock at Circular Quay you're in the heart of it all; White Bay needs a transfer. Either way, a harbour ferry is the best-value 'cruise' in Sydney — ride one to Manly and back.

melbourne.blurb: Melbourne is Australia's culture-and-coffee capital — hidden laneways of street art and espresso bars, grand Victorian arcades, sports temples like the MCG, and the wildlife-rich Great Ocean Road beyond the city.
miss: The laneways' street art and coffee culture
Queen Victoria Market
The Great Ocean Road's Twelve Apostles (long day)
melbourne.tip: The free City Circle tram loops the centre's highlights. The Great Ocean Road is spectacular but a very long day-trip — only attempt it on a late departure, or stay in town for the laneways.

brisbane.blurb: Brisbane is Queensland's sunny, relaxed capital on a looping river — the South Bank parklands with their city-beach lagoon, a koala sanctuary upriver, and the gateway to the Gold Coast and Moreton Bay.
miss: Lone Pine Koala Sanctuary (cuddle a koala)
South Bank's riverside beach and gardens
A CityCat ferry along the Brisbane River
brisbane.tip: The new Brisbane International Cruise Terminal is a fair way from the centre — check transfer options. Lone Pine is the quintessential Brissie experience if you want hands-on wildlife.

cairns.blurb: Cairns is the gateway to two UNESCO wonders side by side — the Great Barrier Reef offshore and the ancient Daintree Rainforest inland. The town's lagoon-lined esplanade is the launch point for reef boats.
miss: A Great Barrier Reef snorkel or dive trip
The Skyrail and scenic railway to Kuranda
The Daintree Rainforest and Cape Tribulation
cairns.tip: The reef is the reason to be here — book a well-reviewed outer-reef trip, as the outer sites are far healthier than the inner ones. Reef boats leave early, so an early call is ideal.

airlie_beach_whitsundays.blurb: Airlie Beach is the gateway to the Whitsundays — 74 island jewels in the Great Barrier Reef, crowned by Whitehaven Beach's blindingly white silica sand and the swirling turquoise of Hill Inlet.
miss: Whitehaven Beach and Hill Inlet lookout
Snorkelling the fringing reefs by boat
A scenic flight over the Heart Reef
airlie_beach_whitsundays.tip: Whitehaven is consistently rated among the world's best beaches and reachable only by boat — a Whitsundays cruise is the essential excursion. A scenic flight over Heart Reef is the splurge.

hobart.blurb: Hobart, Tasmania's harbour capital, pairs colonial sandstone with the wild — the Saturday Salamanca Market, the provocative MONA art museum reached by ferry, and Mount Wellington towering over the town.
miss: MONA (Museum of Old and New Art) by ferry
Salamanca Place's markets and warehouses
The summit drive up Mount Wellington
hobart.tip: MONA is one of the world's most talked-about museums and the catamaran ride there is half the fun. Salamanca Market only runs Saturdays — a bonus if your call lands right.

adelaide_australia.blurb: Adelaide is South Australia's elegant, easy-going capital of parks and churches — but the real draw is the wine, with the world-famous Barossa Valley and the wildlife of Kangaroo Island within reach.
miss: A Barossa or McLaren Vale winery day
Adelaide Central Market's produce
The Adelaide Oval and riverbank precinct
adelaide_australia.tip: A train runs from Outer Harbor into the city in about 30 minutes. Wine country is Adelaide's signature — the Barossa or McLaren Vale make the best day out for anyone who enjoys a drop.

perth-fremantle.blurb: Fremantle is a wonderfully preserved gold-rush port town — a weekend market in a Victorian hall, the sobering convict-era prison, and craft breweries — and the gateway to Perth and the quokkas of Rottnest Island.
miss: Fremantle Prison's convict history
Rottnest Island's quokkas and beaches by ferry
The Fremantle Markets and cappuccino strip
perth-fremantle.tip: 'Freo' is walkable and characterful in its own right — you needn't rush to Perth. Rottnest Island, with its smiling quokkas, is the standout excursion straight from the harbour.

darwin_australia.blurb: Darwin is Australia's tropical Top End capital — a WWII frontline, a gateway to Kakadu and Litchfield national parks, sunset markets on the beach, and a frontier feel closer to Asia than to Sydney.
miss: Litchfield National Park's waterfalls and pools
The Mindil Beach sunset market (dry season)
The WWII oil-storage tunnels and museums
darwin_australia.tip: Kakadu is a huge full-day-plus trip; Litchfield is closer and delivers swimmable waterfalls in a shorter day. Note the wet season (Nov–Apr) closes many swimming spots — check conditions.

auckland.blurb: Auckland, the 'City of Sails', spreads across a volcanic isthmus between two harbours — the Sky Tower over the centre, the vineyards and beaches of Waiheke Island a ferry away, and green volcanic cones dotting the suburbs.
miss: A ferry to Waiheke Island's wineries and beaches
The Sky Tower observation deck (or SkyJump)
The view from a volcanic cone like Mount Eden
auckland.tip: The ship docks right in the city centre. Waiheke Island is the best day out — a 40-minute ferry to vineyards, olive groves and beaches — but book wine tours ahead on busy days.

bay_of_islands_nz.blurb: The Bay of Islands is a subtropical maritime playground of 144 islands in New Zealand's far north — dolphin cruises, the 'Hole in the Rock', and Waitangi, the birthplace of the nation's founding treaty.
miss: A boat cruise to the Hole in the Rock
The Waitangi Treaty Grounds
Dolphin watching, or historic Russell by ferry
bay_of_islands_nz.tip: It's a tender port and the bay is best seen from the water — a boat cruise to the Hole in the Rock, with likely dolphins, is the classic. Historic Russell is a short passenger ferry away.

tauranga_nz.blurb: Tauranga is the port for the Bay of Plenty and, most famously, for Rotorua's geothermal wonderland and Maori culture inland. At the port itself, Mount Maunganui's beach and climbable cone are right there.
miss: Rotorua's geysers, mud pools and Maori culture
The climb up Mauao (Mount Maunganui)
Hobbiton movie set, or a kiwifruit tour
tauranga_nz.tip: Rotorua (about 60–90 min) is the big draw for geothermal and Maori experiences. If you'd rather stay local, Mount Maunganui's beach and summit walk are excellent and right by the ship.

napier_nz.blurb: Napier is the world's most complete Art Deco town, rebuilt in 1930s style after an earthquake — pastel geometric facades, a palm-lined marine parade, and the vineyards of Hawke's Bay all around.
miss: The Art Deco centre's 1930s architecture
A Hawke's Bay winery tour
The Marine Parade and National Aquarium
napier_nz.tip: The Art Deco heart is walkable straight from the port — pick up a self-guided walk map. Hawke's Bay is one of NZ's top wine regions, making a winery tour the natural excursion.

picton_nz.blurb: Picton sits at the head of the Marlborough Sounds' drowned valleys — a gateway to the world-famous Marlborough wine region (think Sauvignon Blanc) and to boat trips and walks among the forested waterways.
miss: A Marlborough wine tour (Sauvignon Blanc country)
A Marlborough Sounds cruise or the Queen Charlotte Track
The Edwin Fox, one of the world's oldest ships
picton_nz.tip: Marlborough is New Zealand's wine powerhouse — a cellar-door tour is the obvious highlight. For scenery over sipping, a Sounds boat cruise or a stretch of the Queen Charlotte Track delivers.

akaroa_nz.blurb: Akaroa is a charming French-founded village on a drowned volcanic crater harbour, a tender ride on the Banks Peninsula — home to the rare, tiny Hector's dolphins and a gateway to Christchurch over the hills.
miss: A harbour cruise to see Hector's dolphins
The French-flavoured village streets and cafés
Christchurch, about 90 minutes away
akaroa_nz.tip: Akaroa is a tender port and often used as the gateway to Christchurch — but the village and its dolphin cruises are lovely in their own right if you'd rather not make the long transfer.

lyttelton_christchurch_nz.blurb: Lyttelton is the port for Christchurch, the South Island's largest city — rebuilding creatively since its 2011 earthquake with a cardboard cathedral and street art, punting on the Avon, and the gateway to the Southern Alps.
miss: Christchurch's Botanic Gardens and punting on the Avon
The Transitional 'Cardboard' Cathedral
The TranzAlpine railway or Arthur's Pass (longer trips)
lyttelton_christchurch_nz.tip: Christchurch is about 20 minutes over (or through) the hills from Lyttelton. The city's earthquake-recovery story — cardboard cathedral, street art, memorials — makes for a moving, walkable day.

port_chalmers_dunedin_nz.blurb: Port Chalmers serves Dunedin, New Zealand's Scottish-heritage city — Victorian and Edwardian architecture, the world's steepest street, and the wildlife-rich Otago Peninsula with albatrosses, penguins and sea lions.
miss: The Otago Peninsula's albatross colony and penguins
Larnach Castle, NZ's only castle
Dunedin's railway station and Baldwin Street
port_chalmers_dunedin_nz.tip: The Otago Peninsula's wildlife — royal albatross, yellow-eyed and little penguins, fur seals — is the standout, and unique to this corner of NZ. Book the albatross centre ahead for guaranteed viewing.

wellington.blurb: Wellington, New Zealand's compact capital, packs in the superb (and free) Te Papa national museum, a cable car to hilltop gardens, a vibrant café and craft-beer scene, and Weta Workshop's film-effects wizardry.
miss: Te Papa, the brilliant national museum (free)
The cable car to the Botanic Garden
Weta Workshop's film-effects tour
wellington.tip: Te Papa is world-class and free — an easy highlight near the waterfront. Wellington is famously windy and walkable; the cable car up and garden stroll down is a lovely low-effort loop.

noumea_new_caledonia.blurb: Nouméa is a slice of France in the South Pacific — baguettes and boules alongside Melanesian Kanak culture, a huge turquoise lagoon (UNESCO-listed), the striking Tjibaou Cultural Centre, and easy beaches.
miss: The Tjibaou Cultural Centre's Kanak architecture
Anse Vata and Baie des Citrons beaches
A glass-bottom boat or snorkel in the lagoon
noumea_new_caledonia.tip: The lagoon is the world's largest and a UNESCO site — snorkelling or a boat trip shows it best. Nouméa mixes French café culture with Pacific island life; the beaches are an easy walk-or-shuttle.

port_vila_vanuatu.blurb: Port Vila is Vanuatu's laid-back capital on a pretty harbour — a lively market, offshore snorkelling islets, blue holes of astonishing clarity in the jungle, and cascading waterfalls a short drive away.
miss: The Mele Cascades waterfall
A blue hole swim in crystal spring water
Hideaway Island's underwater post office by snorkel
port_vila_vanuatu.tip: The blue holes and Mele Cascades are the natural stars — impossibly clear water for swimming. The underwater post office off Hideaway Island is a quirky snorkel novelty.
