mystery_island_vanuatu.blurb: Mystery Island (Inyeug) is an uninhabited speck off southern Vanuatu — a classic castaway day of white sand, snorkelling straight off the beach, and islanders who come over from neighbouring Aneityum to set up market stalls for the day.
miss: Snorkelling the reef right off the beach
Walking the whole tiny island (about 20 minutes)
The island market's crafts and fresh fruit
mystery_island_vanuatu.tip: There's no infrastructure — bring your own snorkel gear, water, sun protection and cash for the stalls. It's a pure beach day, so set expectations to 'blissfully simple'.

suva_fiji.blurb: Suva is Fiji's lively, multicultural capital on the wetter side of the main island — colonial buildings, the excellent Fiji Museum, a bustling municipal market, and rainforest parks with waterfalls nearby.
miss: The Fiji Museum's canoes and cannibal-era relics
The colourful municipal market
Colo-i-Suva rainforest park's pools and trails
suva_fiji.tip: Suva is more workaday capital than beach idyll — the museum and market give real local texture, and the nearby rainforest park offers a cool green escape with swimmable pools.

lautoka_fiji.blurb: Lautoka, Fiji's 'Sugar City' on the drier western coast, is the gateway to the postcard Mamanuca and Yasawa islands, to firewalking Beqa culture, and to the sun-soaked Coral Coast beaches. This is the Fiji of the brochures.
miss: An island-hopping day to the Mamanucas
A traditional Fijian village and kava ceremony
The Garden of the Sleeping Giant orchid gardens
lautoka_fiji.tip: The western side is Fiji's sunny beach heartland — an island transfer to the Mamanucas gives you the classic turquoise-lagoon day. A village visit with a kava ceremony is the cultural counterpart.

papeete_tahiti.blurb: Papeete, on Tahiti, is the capital of French Polynesia — a bustling market of black pearls and tiare flowers, a waterfront of roulottes (food trucks), and a launch point for the lush interior's waterfalls and the neighbouring islands.
miss: The Papeete Market for black pearls and crafts
The roulotte food trucks on the waterfront
The Fautaua Valley waterfalls or a lagoon tour
papeete_tahiti.tip: Tahiti is the gateway rather than the postcard island itself — Moorea and Bora Bora are prettier. In Papeete, the market and the evening roulottes are the authentic local experiences.

moorea_french_polynesia.blurb: Moorea is the heart-shaped island of jagged green peaks and turquoise lagoons just across from Tahiti — snorkelling with rays and reef sharks, a pineapple-planted interior, and the jaw-dropping Belvedere viewpoint over two bays.
miss: Snorkelling with stingrays in the lagoon
The Belvedere Lookout over Cook's and Opunohu bays
A 4x4 tour through the pineapple plantations
moorea_french_polynesia.tip: The lagoon snorkelling — hand-feeding rays in waist-deep water — is Moorea's signature and utterly memorable. It's a tender port, so book water excursions early.

bora_bora_french_polynesia.blurb: Bora Bora is the South Pacific dream — a jade volcanic peak (Mount Otemanu) ringed by a luminous turquoise lagoon and a necklace of palm-fringed motus. Overwater bungalows and world-class lagoon snorkelling define it.
miss: A lagoon tour snorkelling with rays and sharks
The motu picnic and coral gardens
The view of Mount Otemanu from the water
bora_bora_french_polynesia.tip: The lagoon is everything here — a boat-and-snorkel tour is the essential (and only real) way to experience Bora Bora. It's a tender port and excursions sell out, so book well ahead.

pago_pago_american_samoa.blurb: Pago Pago sits in a spectacular volcanic harbour — one of the deepest natural ports in the South Pacific — on American Samoa. Rainforest-clad peaks plunge to the sea, and the National Park of American Samoa protects reef and jungle alike.
miss: The National Park of American Samoa's trails
The dramatic harbour and Rainmaker Mountain
A traditional Samoan village and fiafia culture
pago_pago_american_samoa.tip: The setting — sheer green peaks around a deep harbour — is the star. Samoa is devoutly Christian and traditional; dress modestly, and note Sundays are very quiet.

casablanca_morocco.blurb: Casablanca is Morocco's modern economic heart, home to the colossal Hassan II Mosque rising over the Atlantic — one of the largest in the world, with a minaret to match. It's also the gateway to imperial Rabat and Marrakech.
miss: The Hassan II Mosque (guided tours for non-Muslims)
The Habous quarter and central market
Rabat's kasbah, an hour up the coast
casablanca_morocco.tip: The Hassan II Mosque is one of the few in Morocco open to non-Muslims, by guided tour — book a slot. Marrakech is too far for a comfortable port day; Rabat is the better nearby imperial city.

tangier_morocco.blurb: Tangier guards the Strait of Gibraltar where the Mediterranean meets the Atlantic — a labyrinthine medina and kasbah, a storied bohemian past, and the dramatic Caves of Hercules and Cap Spartel just outside town.
miss: The medina and kasbah's maze of lanes
The Caves of Hercules at Cap Spartel
The Grand Socco and a mint-tea stop
tangier_morocco.tip: A local guide is genuinely useful in Tangier's medina — both to navigate and to ease the persistent attention from touts. Agree terms first. The caves and cape make a scenic short drive out.

dakar.blurb: Dakar, Senegal's vibrant capital on Africa's westernmost point, pulses with music and colour — but its most powerful site is Gorée Island offshore, a moving memorial to the transatlantic slave trade, reached by ferry.
miss: Gorée Island and the House of Slaves
The soaring African Renaissance Monument
The Soumbédioune fish market and craft village
dakar.tip: Gorée Island is the essential and deeply affecting visit — a short ferry from the city. Dakar is lively but can feel chaotic; a guide or organised tour smooths a port day considerably.

cape_town_south_africa.blurb: Cape Town is one of the world's most beautiful cities — Table Mountain rising sheer above the bay, the penguins of Boulders Beach, the Cape of Good Hope, and the winelands of Stellenbosch, all wrapped around a lively waterfront.
miss: The Table Mountain cableway (weather permitting)
Boulders Beach's African penguins
The Cape of Good Hope, or a Stellenbosch wine tour
cape_town_south_africa.tip: Table Mountain is the must, but the cableway closes in high wind — do it the moment the weather's clear rather than saving it. Many ships overnight here; if so, add the winelands or the Cape peninsula.

port_elizabeth_south_africa.blurb: Port Elizabeth (now Gqeberha) is the gateway to malaria-free Big Five safaris — Addo Elephant National Park is close enough for a genuine game drive on a port day, alongside beaches and a colonial-era historic mile.
miss: A game drive in Addo Elephant National Park
The beaches along Algoa Bay
The Donkin Reserve and historic city walk
port_elizabeth_south_africa.tip: Addo is the standout — one of the few places you can do a real Big Five game drive within a port-day's reach, and it's malaria-free. Book the safari excursion; it's why most ships call here.

durban_south_africa.blurb: Durban is South Africa's warm-water playground on the Indian Ocean — the Golden Mile beachfront, the largest Indian community outside India (and superb curry), Zulu culture, and game reserves in the KwaZulu-Natal interior.
miss: The Golden Mile beach promenade
A bunny chow (curry in a bread loaf) — a Durban invention
uShaka Marine World, or a township/Zulu cultural tour
durban_south_africa.tip: Durban's subtropical beachfront is walkable and made for a relaxed day. The Indian-South-African food is a genuine highlight — a bunny chow is the local rite of passage.

walvis_bay_namibia.blurb: Walvis Bay is where the Namib Desert's giant dunes meet the cold Atlantic — flamingo-filled lagoons, a kayak-with-the-seals boat trip at Pelican Point, and the towering apricot dunes around Sandwich Harbour and Sossusvlei.
miss: A Sandwich Harbour 4x4 dune-and-sea drive
A catamaran cruise with seals, dolphins and pelicans
The flamingo lagoon, and nearby Swakopmund
walvis_bay_namibia.tip: The dunes-meet-ocean scenery is extraordinary and unique — a Sandwich Harbour 4x4 trip or a lagoon catamaran are the standout excursions. Colonial Swakopmund nearby is a charming add-on.

port_louis_mauritius.blurb: Port Louis is the capital of Mauritius, a Creole-flavoured harbour city — a lively central market, the Caudan waterfront, botanical gardens with giant water lilies, and the island's volcanic peaks and beaches beyond.
miss: The Central Market's spices and street food
Pamplemousses Botanical Garden's giant lilies
The Caudan Waterfront, or a beach at Grand Baie
port_louis_mauritius.tip: Port Louis' market and waterfront make an easy walkable morning. Mauritius' famous beaches need a drive — Grand Baie in the north or the south-west coast are the pick for sand and lagoon.

victoria_seychelles.blurb: Victoria, on Mahé, is one of the world's smallest capitals — a colourful market, a miniature replica of London's Big Ben, and Hindu and colonial landmarks — set on an island of granite peaks and some of the planet's finest beaches.
miss: Beau Vallon or Anse Intendance beaches
The Sir Selwyn Selwyn-Clarke Market
The Morne Seychellois national park viewpoints
victoria_seychelles.tip: The Seychelles is all about the beaches — Mahé's Anse Intendance and Beau Vallon are stunning. Victoria itself takes barely an hour to see, so head for the sand or a nearby-island snorkel trip.

mombasa_kenya.blurb: Mombasa is Kenya's coastal gateway — a Swahili old town of carved doors and spice markets, the Portuguese Fort Jesus guarding the harbour, white-sand beaches, and, for the ambitious, safari in Tsavo National Park.
miss: Fort Jesus, the UNESCO Portuguese fortress
The Old Town's Swahili lanes and markets
A Tsavo safari, or Haller Park's wildlife
mombasa_kenya.tip: A full Tsavo safari is a long, early day — Haller Park (a rehabilitated quarry now full of giraffe and hippo) is a much closer wildlife fix. Fort Jesus and the old town are an easy cultural half-day.

zanzibar.blurb: Zanzibar's Stone Town is a UNESCO maze of Swahili, Arab and Indian heritage — carved doors, a former slave market, spice-trade history and the birthplace of Freddie Mercury — fringed by turquoise water and spice plantations.
miss: Stone Town's labyrinth and the former slave market
A spice-plantation tour (cloves, nutmeg, vanilla)
Prison Island's giant tortoises by boat
zanzibar.tip: Stone Town is walkable but genuinely maze-like — getting a little lost is part of it, though a guide helps with the history. A spice tour is the classic Zanzibar excursion. Dress modestly; the island is largely Muslim.
